title: The David Lynch Episode
format: Sean Fennessey and Ringer contributor Adam Nayman mark 30 years of Twin Peaks: Fire Walk with Me and 25 of Lost Highway with a new Criterion restoration, then rank all ten Lynch features.

films.title: Eraserhead
films.note: Nayman calls it arguably the cult film of the last 50 years in America, and the thing Lynch was already semi-famous for before Blue Velvet.

films.title: The Elephant Man
films.note: The Mel Brooks-produced follow-up that got Lynch his first Oscar nominations and nearly landed him Return of the Jedi.

films.title: Dune
films.note: The disastrous studio project both agree derailed his trajectory as an artist, even as they admit to liking pieces of it.

films.title: Blue Velvet
films.note: The film that made him seriously famous; Nayman argues it's been picked over so thoroughly it's nearly immune to analysis at this point.

segments.heading: Twin Peaks: Fire Walk with Me at 30

films.title: Twin Peaks: Fire Walk with Me
films.note: Nayman calls it his most primal movie and top tier for him personally. Both trace how Lynch built a prequel forcing viewers to watch what actually happened to Laura Palmer, largely without the show's original cast, and land on it as a horror film that's also deep melodrama.

films.title: Lost Highway
films.note: The pair frame it around voyeurism and Bill Pullman's line 'I like to remember things my own way' as a mission statement for Lynch, and note the Robert Blake role riffing on the O.J. Simpson case.

films.title: Mulholland Drive
films.note: Discussed as the sunnier half of the same Hollywood story Lost Highway tells, with Naomi Watts singled out as the best-directed performance in any Lynch film.

films.title: Inland Empire
films.note: Called the unofficial third part of the Hollywood trilogy with Lost Highway and Mulholland Drive, and probably his least-seen film.

films.title: Mulholland Drive
films.note: No. 1 for both. Nayman: it's the movie where culture decided he'd come out the other end of his unfashionability, and it was a date-night movie he and his now-wife dissected for hours.

films.title: Blue Velvet
films.note: No. 2. The one that made him seriously famous as a filmmaker.

films.title: The Elephant Man
films.note: No. 3. Nayman's sentimental favorite even knowing it won't win: John Hurt's performance and Lynch making the conventional feel unconventional.

films.title: Twin Peaks: Fire Walk with Me
films.note: No. 4, the spot they agree feels right after an hour of arguing for it.

films.title: The Straight Story
films.note: No. 5. Nayman would rank it higher; Sean notes one site once ranked it below the Pokemon movie in a 1999 roundup.

films.title: Lost Highway
films.note: No. 6, negotiated down from Nayman's initial instinct to put it at 7.

films.title: Eraserhead
films.note: No. 7. Both admit a recent rewatch wasn't much fun, precisely because its influence has been so thoroughly absorbed.

films.title: Inland Empire
films.note: No. 8. Nayman: high on obsession, lower on pleasure, though he rates it a bit above Sean.

films.title: Wild at Heart
films.note: No. 9. Nayman's clearly stated least favorite, despite its Palme d'Or and defenders who'd rank it near the top.

Twin Peaks: Fire Walk with Me.title: Twin Peaks: Fire Walk with Me
